fit_assessment.primary_problem: Separating a 97-year-old, $24B company with 58,000+ employees across 17 countries into two independent public companies — each needing its own leadership, culture, operating model, board, and strategic identity — while executing multi-hundred-million restructuring, navigating activist pressure, and managing volatile tariff environment. This is a leadership, organizational design, and execution-under-pressure problem.
fit_assessment.best_capability_fit: Organizational design — designing two standalone operating models from one. Team of Teams — both entities need cross-functional alignment rapidly (automotive spans 10,800+ locations across 17 countries). Leadership development — new teams moving from 'division heads' to 'independent company leaders.' Culture change — 'stuck in the 80s' culture must now move at separation speed.
likely_objections: Have McKinsey/Bain/BCG advising on separation structure
Managing costs tightly
New CPO handling people/culture internally
Elliott watching spend closely
competitive_landscape: PwC — Laurie Schupmann (39yr PwC veteran) just nominated to board

McChrystal differentiator: execution and leadership, not strategy decks. Separating a unified command into two independent operating units while maintaining effectiveness is precisely what JSOC has done.
fit_assessment.rating_rationale: Tier 1 prospect — large industrial undergoing most transformative event in 97-year history. Corporate separation + activist pressure + new leadership + culture challenges + global complexity. Timing ideal: separation announced but execution just beginning (Q1 2027 target).

conversation_entries.opening_question: You're standing up two $10B+ public companies from one 97-year-old organization in ~12 months. What's been harder — separating the systems and processes, or separating the identity and culture?

With Elliott on your board and the separation clock ticking, how are you thinking about the leadership bench for two independent companies — especially the layer below C-suite where execution actually happens?

Your Glassdoor reviews describe a culture that's been stable for decades. Stability is a strength — until you need 58,000 people to move in a new direction fast. How are you shifting that culture without losing what made it work for 97 years?

dossiers.recommended_meeting_framing: Frame around separation execution, not strategy. 'The strategy for why you're separating is clear. The hard part is getting 58,000 people across 17 countries to operate as two distinct, high-performing organizations while it's happening. That's a leadership and organizational challenge — the kind of problem we were built to solve.' The military analogy of separating a unified command into two independent operating units is not forced — it is the GPC situation.

signals.description: Completed merger with TruckPro (Oct 2025), forming the largest independent heavy-duty aftermarket distributor in North America with 450+ locations, 110+ service centers, and 6 distribution centers. Dual PE ownership: American Securities + Platinum Equity.
New CEO Tom Greco appointed July 2025 — former CEO of Advance Auto Parts and Frito-Lay North America with 40+ years of Fortune 500 leadership. Replaced interim CEO Stefan Crafton.

Post-merger integration of two large distribution networks under new CEO, requiring culture alignment, operational harmonization, and organizational redesign across 450+ locations and ~6,500 employees.

prospect.summary: FleetPride is in the early stages of a major post-merger integration after combining with TruckPro under a new CEO with deep transformation experience. The company must unify two cultures, rationalize overlapping operations, and build a cohesive leadership operating model across a geographically distributed, frontline-heavy workforce — a classic McChrystal engagement scenario.
prospect.entry_point: CEO Tom Greco — new to FleetPride (July 2025), leading the most consequential integration in the company's history. His track record at Advance Auto Parts shows he values operational discipline and culture transformation, both McChrystal strengths.
prospect.conversation_hook: FleetPride just completed the largest merger in heavy-duty aftermarket history and is in the critical first year of integration. Greco has publicly stated integration must 'start with people and culture, not spreadsheets' — that language maps directly to McChrystal's Team of Teams operating model.

dossiers.ownership_structure: Private, dual PE-backed — American Securities (majority owner, acquired FleetPride) and Platinum Equity (brought TruckPro into the merger). Mark Lovett (American Securities Managing Director) serves as Board Chair.
dossiers.geographic_footprint: 450+ locations across the United States and Canada, including 110+ service centers and 6 distribution centers. Concentrated in major trucking corridors and metropolitan areas. Houston, Dallas, and San Antonio presence strengthened by 2025 OTR Fleet Service acquisition.
dossiers.financial_health: Estimated ~$3B combined revenue (some sources cite up to $5B post-merger). Moody's noted 'improving operating margin' and expects high-single-digit revenue growth, with EBITDA margin increasing slightly. However, leverage remains elevated at ~6x debt/EBITDA — Moody's previously flagged 'very high leverage, low interest coverage, and weak liquidity attributed to ongoing negative free cash flow.' Debt was restructured in late 2025 with OHA (T. Rowe Price) leading private financing to support the merger. Moody's subsequently withdrew its rating after debt repayment and assigned a stable outlook. The financial picture is stabilizing but still fragile — the integration must deliver cost synergies and revenue growth to service the debt load. PE owners will be focused on a 3–5 year exit timeline, which creates urgency for operational improvement and EBITDA expansion.

key_contacts.name: Tom Greco
key_contacts.title: Chief Executive Officer
key_contacts.background: 40+ years Fortune 500 leadership. CEO of Advance Auto Parts (2016–2023), where he led a multi-year transformation delivering 5 consecutive years of comparable sales growth. Previously CEO of Frito-Lay North America (PepsiCo) for 30+ years. MBA from Richard Ivey School of Business (Western University). Public company board experience.
key_contacts.notes: No military/government background identified. Greco is a proven transformation CEO who values operational discipline and frontline engagement. Publicly stated that FleetPride–TruckPro integration must 'start with people and culture, not spreadsheets.'
key_contacts.priority_rationale: Economic buyer and decision-maker. New CEO leading the most consequential integration in the company's history. His transformation track record at Advance Auto Parts and stated focus on people-and-culture-first integration aligns directly with McChrystal capabilities. He has the mandate, the budget authority, and a proven appetite for organizational change.

key_contacts.title: President, TruckPro Division

key_contacts.background: Operator with deep heavy-duty aftermarket experience. Led TruckPro through Platinum Equity ownership period and growth to 150+ locations.
key_contacts.notes: Reports to Greco. Key integration counterpart — his buy-in is essential for cultural alignment between legacy TruckPro and FleetPride teams. Could be a champion or a blocker.

key_contacts.notes: Board-level influence on strategic spend. PE chairs typically want to see measurable ROI from advisory engagements — frame McChrystal work in terms of integration speed, retention, and EBITDA protection.

dossiers.org_structure: Historically hierarchical, branch-based distribution model. Post-merger, FleetPride operates a dual-track structure: Tom Greco as CEO with Chuck Broadus leading the TruckPro division reporting to him. The 450+ location footprint is inherently decentralized operationally but has traditionally been managed with centralized control from headquarters — a tension that the merger will amplify.
dossiers.culture_signals: Glassdoor rating of 2.4/5 (based on 316 reviews) with only 21% of employees recommending the company — a significant red flag. Key themes in employee reviews: 'Executive leadership is a nightmare,' 'You feel like a number here, there is no company culture,' 'Nepotism runs rampant,' 'No work-life balance,' and 'They like to micromanage.' Work-life balance rated 2.2/5, culture and values 2.2/5, career opportunities 2.3/5. These scores reflect the pre-Greco, pre-merger FleetPride — but they represent the cultural baseline that must be transformed. The TruckPro workforce brings its own distinct culture, creating a two-cultures-colliding dynamic. Greco's stated focus on people-first integration suggests he recognizes the culture deficit. [INFERRED] The blue-collar, distributed workforce (branch managers, mechanics, parts counter staff) requires a culture change approach that works at the frontline level — not just C-suite workshops. McChrystal's methodology is well-suited to this.

trigger_events.relevance: Significant debt restructuring — Moody's noted 'very high leverage' and debt/EBITDA near 6x. Financial pressure means the integration must deliver results quickly; failed integration = existential risk.

trigger_events.relevance: Industry-wide disruption compounds internal integration challenges. FleetPride must simultaneously integrate TruckPro AND adapt to structural market changes — the kind of VUCA environment where McChrystal's framework is most compelling.

fit_assessment.primary_problem: Post-merger integration of two large, geographically distributed organizations with distinct cultures, overlapping operations, and a frontline-heavy workforce — all under significant financial leverage and with a new CEO who must establish authority quickly.
fit_assessment.best_capability_fit: Team of Teams operating model implementation. FleetPride's challenge is textbook McChrystal: a newly combined organization with 450+ locations needs to build shared consciousness, break down legacy silos (FleetPride vs. TruckPro), and empower distributed frontline leaders to execute autonomously while staying aligned. Greco's stated philosophy that integration 'starts with people and culture' is the exact entry point for McChrystal's approach.
likely_objections: PE owners (American Securities + Platinum Equity) may resist advisory spend given high leverage (6x debt/EBITDA) — must frame as integration risk mitigation and EBITDA protection, not discretionary consulting

FleetPride may already have integration consultants from PE firms' preferred vendor lists (McKinsey, Bain, AlixPartners) — McChrystal needs to differentiate on culture/leadership vs. operational/financial integration
Greco's 40+ years of Fortune 500 experience may make him skeptical of outside advisors — need to position McChrystal as a peer-level partner, not a typical consultancy

Heavy-duty auto parts distribution may feel 'too blue collar' for McChrystal's brand — but the distributed frontline workforce and high-stakes operating environment (fleet downtime = customer revenue loss) are actually ideal fit factors
competitive_landscape: McKinsey Organization Practice — PE firms often default to McKinsey for post-merger integration; however, McKinsey's approach is typically top-down and analytical, not culture-first
Bain & Company — strong PE relationships (both American Securities and Platinum Equity are Bain clients); likely has existing relationship for deal diligence
AlixPartners — common PE-backed integration advisor, but focused on operational/financial restructuring, not leadership and culture
Korn Ferry — could compete on leadership assessment and executive coaching; lacks McChrystal's operating model and team-based transformation methodology
Internal PE operating partners — both American Securities and Platinum Equity have operating teams that may be driving integration priorities
fit_assessment.rating_rationale: FleetPride presents a compelling engagement opportunity: new transformation CEO + massive post-merger integration + distributed frontline workforce + high financial leverage creating urgency. Greco's public statements about people-first integration align perfectly with McChrystal's methodology. The primary risk is PE budget sensitivity, but the integration stakes are high enough that a well-framed proposal (integration insurance, not discretionary consulting) should resonate.

conversation_entries.opening_question: You've said publicly that the FleetPride–TruckPro integration has to start with people and culture, not spreadsheets. Eight months in, what's been the hardest part of getting 6,500 people across 450 locations to feel like one team?
conversation_entries.framing: Validate Greco's stated philosophy, then pivot to McChrystal's experience building shared consciousness in large, distributed organizations that were forced to integrate quickly (JSOC parallel).

conversation_entries.opening_question: With dual PE ownership and significant leverage, how are you thinking about the balance between integration speed and getting the culture right — especially when every quarter matters to your board?
conversation_entries.framing: Acknowledge the financial pressure without being presumptuous. Position McChrystal as having helped organizations accelerate integration timelines without sacrificing cultural alignment — integration speed as a competitive advantage, not a tradeoff.

conversation_entries.opening_question: The heavy-duty aftermarket is facing tariff disruptions, electrification shifts, and digital transformation all at once. How is FleetPride building the organizational agility to absorb those external shocks while simultaneously integrating TruckPro?
conversation_entries.framing: Frame the VUCA environment — FleetPride faces both internal complexity (integration) and external disruption (market shifts). McChrystal's Team of Teams model was built for exactly this kind of compound uncertainty.

dossiers.recommended_meeting_framing: Position as a peer-level conversation about leading large-scale integration in distributed, frontline-heavy organizations. Lead with the JSOC parallel: General McChrystal faced the same challenge of unifying disparate units with distinct cultures into a single, cohesive force capable of moving faster than the threat environment. Avoid 'consulting pitch' framing — Greco has 40 years of experience and will reject anything that feels generic. Offer a specific, concrete insight about post-merger culture integration (e.g., a 90-day shared consciousness accelerator) rather than a broad capabilities overview. If possible, reference a specific PE-backed integration McChrystal has supported.

signals.description: New CEO Patrick Kelleher (ex-DHL) appointed August 2025, replacing retiring Malcolm Wilson. New COO Bart Beeks (ex-CEVA) started January 2026 in a newly created role. Michael Jacobs (ex-Ferguson) hired as President Americas & APAC in November 2025.

CEO Kelleher initiated sweeping management restructuring within weeks of arrival. Eliminated CRO role (Richard Cawston departing March 2026), flattened European reporting to CEO direct, created new COO position, and appointed regional president for Americas/APAC. Three senior leaders replaced in 6 months.

Completed acquisition of UK logistics firm Wincanton in 2024-2025. UK CMA cleared deal with conditions (divest some grocery contracts). Integration commenced Q3 2025 with $50-70M annual synergies targeted by 2026.

Major technology transformation: 20,000+ robots deployed globally, AI platform GXO IQ rolling out across 1,200+ operations, humanoid robot deployments with Agility Robotics and Reflex Robotics (industry firsts). Automation and digital transformation underpins margin expansion strategy.

prospect.summary: World's largest pure-play contract logistics company undergoing comprehensive leadership overhaul and organizational restructuring under new CEO. Three C-suite changes in 6 months, Wincanton integration in progress, and massive automation transformation create a textbook 'new leader needs to align a global organization' scenario.
prospect.entry_point: CEO Patrick Kelleher (new leader architecting organizational change) or COO Bart Beeks (newly created role, charged with standardizing global operations across 1,200+ sites)
prospect.conversation_hook: Kelleher has replaced or restructured nearly every direct report within 6 months — the urgency to build a cohesive leadership team and shared operating model across 130,000 people in 27 countries is immediate.

dossiers.ownership_structure: Public (NYSE: GXO). Spun off from XPO Logistics in August 2021. No controlling shareholder; institutional investor base.
dossiers.geographic_footprint: Operations in approximately 27 countries across North America, Europe, and Asia-Pacific. 1,200+ warehouse and logistics facilities. Major presence in US, UK (expanded via Wincanton acquisition), Continental Europe, and select APAC markets.
dossiers.financial_health:
**Revenue**: $13.2B FY2025 (record), up 12.5% YoY from $11.7B in FY2024, driven by Wincanton acquisition and 3.5% organic growth. Q4 2025 revenue of $3.5B (+7.9% YoY).

**Profitability**: Adjusted EBITDA of $881M FY2025 (up from $815M), but GAAP diluted EPS dropped sharply from $1.12 to $0.28 — reflecting integration costs, higher interest expense, and one-time items. Adjusted EPS of $2.52 provides a cleaner picture.

**2026 Guidance**: Organic revenue growth of 4-5%, adjusted EBITDA of $930-970M (8% growth), adjusted EPS of $2.85-$3.15 (20% growth). Company expects $50-70M in annual Wincanton synergies by 2026.

**Analyst Sentiment**: Consensus Strong Buy from 11 analysts, average price target $67.91. Investors are cautiously optimistic on margin expansion and integration execution but watching leverage and leadership transition risk.

**Key Pressures**: Integration execution risk on Wincanton, elevated leverage from acquisitions, EPS decline despite revenue growth, multiple simultaneous leadership transitions. **Key Tailwinds**: Record new business wins ($1B+ for third consecutive year), automation-driven productivity gains, margin expansion trajectory, strong 2026 pipeline ($774M incremental revenue).

key_contacts.name: Patrick Kelleher
key_contacts.title: Chief Executive Officer
key_contacts.background: 33 years in global supply chain. Former CEO North America at DHL Supply Chain (Deutsche Post DHL). Led M&A transactions, robotics deployments (Boston Dynamics Stretch), and major operational transformations at DHL. Metrics-driven leader focused on execution and innovation.
key_contacts.notes: First external CEO in GXO's history as independent company. Immediately initiated sweeping management restructuring. Operational background suggests comfort with execution-oriented advisory.
key_contacts.priority_rationale: Economic buyer. New CEO actively reshaping the organization — replaced CRO, created COO role, hired new Americas president. Clearly driving a mandate to build 'his team' and a new operating rhythm. Open to external advisory that accelerates transformation without adding bureaucracy.

key_contacts.name: Bart Beeks
key_contacts.title: Chief Operating Officer
key_contacts.background: Career at CEVA Logistics, rising from intern to COO. Managed operations in 170+ countries, led multiple acquisition integrations, drove operating margin improvements. Based in Eindhoven, Netherlands.
key_contacts.notes: Newly created role focused on standardizing global execution. Charged with improving productivity, efficiency, and customer value across 1,200+ sites. Key integration leader for Wincanton.
key_contacts.priority_rationale: Internal champion. His mandate — 'standardize global execution' across a fragmented, recently-acquired operation — is the exact problem McChrystal's Team of Teams operating model solves. New to the organization and building his own playbook.

key_contacts.name: Michael Jacobs
key_contacts.title: President, Americas & Asia Pacific
key_contacts.background: 30+ years in supply chain operations. Former SVP Supply Chain at Ferguson Enterprises where he led supply chain transformation including robotics and AI. Previously led global supply chain at Toys 'R' Us across 33 countries and worldwide distribution at Keurig.
key_contacts.notes: Based in Dallas. Brings shipper-side perspective (Ferguson, Keurig, Toys R Us) vs. GXO's 3PL heritage. Experienced in automation and AI-driven supply chain transformation.

key_contacts.name: Baris Oran
key_contacts.title: Chief Financial Officer
key_contacts.background: Finance executive. Joined from XPO Logistics during spinoff. Oversees financial strategy, capital allocation, and investor relations.
key_contacts.notes: Longest-tenured C-suite member from original GXO leadership. Provides institutional continuity amid leadership transitions. Key to budget approval for advisory engagements.

key_contacts.name: Mark Manduca
key_contacts.title: Chief Investment Officer
key_contacts.background: Investment and strategy background. Oversees M&A pipeline and strategic investments including automation technology partnerships.
key_contacts.notes: Drove Wincanton acquisition and Clipper Logistics deal. Key voice in GXO's growth-through-acquisition strategy.

dossiers.org_structure: Historically regional/hierarchical structure inherited from XPO spinoff. Currently undergoing active restructuring under new CEO Kelleher: European reporting flattened to CEO direct (eliminating CRO layer), new COO role created for global operational standardization, Americas/APAC consolidated under single president. Shifting from decentralized regional fiefdoms toward a more matrixed, globally standardized operating model.
dossiers.culture_signals:
**Glassdoor**: 3.4/5.0 overall rating (2,082 reviews). Culture & Values rated 3.2/5.0. 61% would recommend to a friend.

**Positive signals**: Strong benefits, learning opportunities, sense of belonging in some locations. Company invests in automation training.

**Concerning patterns**: Recurring themes of 'bad management,' 'favoritism,' and disconnect between corporate vision and warehouse-floor reality. Reviews describe a 'startup culture that prioritizes relentless output over meaningful work' and 'upper management is extremely biased.' Long hours and shifting schedules are common complaints.

**McChrystal relevance**: The gap between corporate headquarters (Greenwich, CT) and 1,200+ warehouse operations is a classic 'shared consciousness' problem. Mid-level management quality appears inconsistent — exactly the leadership layer where Team of Teams operating rhythms create the most impact. The Wincanton integration adds another cultural layer (UK heritage vs. US-centric GXO).

trigger_events.relevance: Creating a new COO role signals that operational fragmentation is a recognized problem. Beeks' mandate to standardize 1,200+ sites is a natural McChrystal Team of Teams engagement.

trigger_events.relevance: Technology transformation at this scale requires aligned leadership, cross-functional collaboration, and change management — areas where warehouse-level adoption can stall without shared consciousness across sites.

fit_assessment.primary_problem: A new CEO is simultaneously restructuring leadership, integrating a major acquisition (Wincanton), and driving automation transformation across 1,200+ facilities in 27 countries — without an established operating model or cohesive leadership team to execute it all. The organization risks fragmentation as three major change initiatives compete for attention and new leaders (CEO, COO, Americas President) try to build alignment with inherited teams.
fit_assessment.best_capability_fit: Team of Teams operating model implementation. GXO's challenge is textbook: a large, geographically dispersed organization with siloed regional structures needs to move faster, share information better, and execute consistently across 130,000 people. The new COO's explicit mandate to 'standardize global execution' is precisely what the Team of Teams model delivers — shared consciousness, empowered execution, and cross-functional alignment without centralizing all decision-making.
likely_objections: 'We're an operations company — we need operational consultants, not leadership advisors.' Counter: McChrystal's model was built in operational environments where execution speed and coordination across dispersed teams was life-or-death. The principles translate directly.

'We just hired a new COO to solve this.' Counter: Beeks needs a framework and facilitation to accelerate alignment, not replace his role. McChrystal accelerates what good operators are already trying to do.
'Budget is tight — EPS dropped 75% year-over-year.' Counter: The $50-70M Wincanton synergy target alone justifies a $500K-$1M advisory engagement if it accelerates integration by even one quarter.

'We've got 20,000 robots — our transformation is technological, not organizational.' Counter: Technology adoption at scale is an organizational challenge. The robots work; the question is whether 130,000 people across 27 countries are aligned on how to deploy and optimize them.
competitive_landscape: McKinsey Operations Practice — likely engaged on Wincanton integration synergy capture and supply chain optimization
Korn Ferry — potential leadership assessment and executive onboarding for the multiple new C-suite hires
Bain & Company — may be advising on post-merger integration playbook given PE heritage (XPO/GXO has PE DNA)
Gartner Supply Chain — technology advisory and benchmarking for automation strategy
Internal transformation team — GXO likely has an internal PMO managing integration; McChrystal would need to complement, not compete with, this team
fit_assessment.rating_rationale: Meets 4 of 5 Strong criteria: (1) Active trigger events within last 6 months (multiple C-suite changes, restructuring, Wincanton integration); (2) Clear economic buyers identified (Kelleher, Beeks); (3) Budget exists ($13.2B revenue, known transformation spend, $50-70M synergy target); (4) Organizational urgency (new CEO mandate, 75% EPS decline, three simultaneous transformations). Competitive barrier is moderate — consulting firms are likely present but McChrystal's differentiated Team of Teams model has no direct analog in the logistics advisory space.

conversation_entries.opening_question: You've restructured nearly every senior leadership role in your first 6 months — what's your framework for getting this new team operating as one unit across 27 countries before the market expects results?
conversation_entries.framing: Position McChrystal as the firm that helps new leadership teams build shared consciousness and operating rhythm at speed — not strategy consultants, but the team that helps leaders lead differently.

conversation_entries.opening_question: Your new COO is tasked with standardizing global execution across 1,200+ sites — how are you thinking about the balance between centralized standards and local autonomy in markets as different as the UK, Continental Europe, and the Americas?
conversation_entries.framing: This opens the door to Team of Teams — the tension between standardization and empowered execution is the core problem the model solves. Reference General McChrystal's experience standardizing operations across Joint Special Operations Command's geographically dispersed units.

conversation_entries.opening_question: With Wincanton integration underway and 20,000+ robots deployed, how confident are you that your mid-level leaders — the site managers and regional directors — are aligned on priorities and empowered to make the right calls daily?
conversation_entries.framing: Shift the conversation from C-suite strategy to mid-level execution. McChrystal's model is about pushing decision-making down — and GXO's warehouse-level leadership is where transformation succeeds or fails.

dossiers.recommended_meeting_framing: Position as a peer conversation between leaders who've built high-performing organizations in complex, dispersed operating environments. Lead with General McChrystal's experience unifying special operations across multiple countries and commands — the parallel to GXO's 27-country, 1,200-site challenge is immediate and credible. Avoid 'consulting pitch' framing; Kelleher is an operator who will respect direct, experience-based dialogue over frameworks and decks.

signals.description: Silver Lake completed $25B take-private of Endeavor in March 2025 — the largest PE take-private in media/entertainment history. Company delisted from NYSE.
Ari Emanuel stepped down as CEO; Mark Shapiro became President & Managing Partner. Patrick Whitesell exited entirely, taking WME Football with him. Emanuel moved to Executive Chairman.

Company rebranded from Endeavor to WME Group. Massive asset divestitures: OpenBet/IMG Arena sold for $450M; IMG, PBR, On Location transferred to TKO in $3.25B all-stock deal. Remaining entity focused on talent agency (WME), 160over90, IMG Licensing.

Post-divestiture WME Group must redefine identity and operating model as a leaner, talent-centric business under Silver Lake ownership, while Shapiro simultaneously runs TKO (UFC/WWE). Dual-mandate leadership creates organizational complexity.

prospect.summary: WME Group is undergoing one of the most significant organizational transformations in entertainment industry history: a $25B PE take-private, CEO transition, corporate rebrand, and massive asset divestitures — all simultaneously. The new leadership must integrate a fragmented portfolio under a fresh identity while Silver Lake demands operational efficiency and value creation.
prospect.entry_point: Mark Shapiro, President & Managing Partner — newly installed leader of WME Group who must build a cohesive culture across the remaining portfolio while also running TKO. His dual role makes organizational effectiveness an existential priority.
prospect.conversation_hook: With Ari Emanuel stepping back, Patrick Whitesell departing, and half the company's assets divested to TKO, Shapiro is effectively building a new company from the shell of Endeavor. The leadership transition and organizational redesign challenge is immediate and acute.

dossiers.legal_name: Endeavor Group Holdings, Inc. (d/b/a WME Group)
dossiers.ownership_structure: Private — PE-backed by Silver Lake (majority owner since March 2025 take-private). Key equity holders include Silver Lake, Mubadala Investment Company, Goldman Sachs Asset Management, and rolled-over management equity from Emanuel, Shapiro, and others. WME Group retains 61% stake in publicly traded TKO Group Holdings (NYSE: TKO).
dossiers.geographic_footprint: Global operations across 30+ countries. Primary offices in Beverly Hills (HQ), New York City, Nashville, London, and Sydney. The WME talent agency operates primarily in the U.S. with growing international reach. 160over90 and IMG Licensing have broader global footprints. TKO (majority-owned subsidiary) has significant international operations through UFC and WWE.
dossiers.financial_health:
**Revenue**: Estimated ~$6B consolidated (WME Group + 61% TKO stake). TKO alone reported $4.735B in 2025 revenue with guidance for $5.675-5.775B in 2026. WME Group's standalone revenue (agency commissions, 160over90, IMG Licensing) is [INFERRED] in the $1-1.5B range after divestitures.

**Profitability**: TKO generated $1.585B adjusted EBITDA in 2025 (up 47% YoY) and $546M net income. WME Group standalone profitability is unavailable as a private company, but talent agencies typically operate at 15-25% margins [INFERRED].

**Key Pressures**: (1) Significant debt load from $25B leveraged buyout — Silver Lake will demand efficient capital allocation and margin expansion. (2) Shapiro's dual-mandate across WME Group and TKO creates bandwidth constraints at the top. (3) Post-divestiture identity crisis — remaining WME Group must justify its standalone existence. **Tailwinds**: (1) TKO's explosive growth ($7.7B UFC Paramount deal, $5B WWE Netflix deal) generates massive cash flow. (2) Silver Lake's track record of scaling portfolio companies provides operational expertise. (3) Entertainment industry consolidation creates opportunities for a well-capitalized talent agency. dossiers.org_structure: Historically hierarchical and personality-driven — built around Ari Emanuel's centralized authority and agent-principal relationship model. The talent agency business is inherently structured around individual agent relationships (each agent operates as a quasi-independent business). Post-rebrand, WME Group encompasses three main business units: WME (talent agency), 160over90 (marketing agency), and IMG Licensing — each with distinct cultures and operating models. Shapiro's dual role as head of both WME Group and TKO adds a matrixed reporting complexity. The co-chairman structure (Muirhead + Weitz) under Shapiro is a new governance experiment without a clear precedent at the company.
dossiers.culture_signals:
**Glassdoor Data**: WME rated 3.4/5 for culture and values; 3.0/5 for work-life balance; Endeavor parent rated 3.5/5 overall (376 reviews). Only 66% of WME employees recommend it to a friend.

**Recurring Themes**: (1) 'Upper management could not care less about employees' — consistent complaint about exploitation and lack of investment in people. (2) 'Pay your dues' mentality — outdated hazing culture where junior employees endure poor treatment as a rite of passage. (3) Minimal benefits — low vacation days, no 401k match, discouraged PTO usage. (4) 'Huge egos' and hyper-competitive internal dynamics. (5) High career opportunity rating (4.0/5) suggests people stay for access and resume value, not because they're treated well.

**McChrystal Relevance**: The culture profile is a textbook case for McChrystal's transformation work — a hierarchical, ego-driven organization where information hoarding and internal competition undermine collective effectiveness. The PE transition creates both the mandate and the window to address these issues. Shapiro's stated emphasis on 'clear expectations' and 'results-driven culture' suggests he recognizes the problem. The challenge: agents are the revenue generators and resist process-driven transformation, similar to how special operators initially resisted JSOC's information-sharing mandates.

fit_assessment.primary_problem: WME Group is undergoing simultaneous CEO transition, corporate identity rebrand, and radical portfolio simplification under new PE ownership — requiring a cohesive organizational operating model to be built essentially from scratch. The dual-mandate leadership (Shapiro running both WME Group and TKO) creates alignment and prioritization challenges across the organization. Legacy culture problems (hierarchical 'pay your dues' mentality, low employee engagement, agent-centric power structures) are incompatible with the leaner, more integrated organization Silver Lake needs.
fit_assessment.best_capability_fit: Team of Teams operating model implementation and organizational design/transformation. WME Group's challenge is a textbook case for McChrystal's approach: a formerly sprawling, personality-driven conglomerate must become an aligned, adaptive, cross-functional organization under new leadership. The Team of Teams framework directly addresses the silos between WME agency, 160over90, and IMG Licensing — entities that historically operated as independent fiefdoms. Leadership development for Shapiro's expanded team and culture change initiatives would be natural follow-ons.
likely_objections: 'We're an entertainment company — military leadership frameworks don't apply here.' Counter: the VUCA conditions WME Group faces (disrupted industry, new ownership, leadership transition) are precisely the environment McChrystal's model was built for.

'We don't have time for a multi-month engagement — we're in execution mode.' Counter: McChrystal's approach is designed for organizations in motion, not academic retreats. The Team of Teams model was developed during active combat operations.

'Ari/the agents won't buy into process-driven transformation.' Counter: the engagement targets Shapiro and the co-chairs, not individual agents. The goal is organizational infrastructure that supports (not constrains) the relationship business.

'Our culture is unique — outsiders don't understand Hollywood.' Counter: McChrystal Group has worked with high-ego, relationship-driven organizations in defense, finance, and sports. The dynamics are more universal than they appear.
competitive_landscape: McKinsey Organization Practice — likely already advising Silver Lake on post-acquisition operating model
Bain & Company — Silver Lake's preferred consulting partner for PE portfolio companies

fit_assessment.rating_rationale: Strong fit despite Tier 3 industry classification. The combination of PE take-private, simultaneous CEO departure + new leader installation, corporate rebrand, and massive asset divestitures creates one of the most complex organizational transformation challenges in recent entertainment industry history. The ICP score of 92.7 reflects the density of transformation signals. Shapiro's publicly stated emphasis on 'clear expectations' and 'results-driven culture' suggests philosophical alignment with McChrystal's approach. The $6B revenue and ~10,000 employees place WME Group squarely in the ICP sweet spot for company size.

conversation_entries.opening_question: You're running two organizations simultaneously — WME Group and TKO — each with its own culture, priorities, and stakeholders. conversation_entries.framing: Position McChrystal's experience helping military leaders manage complex, multi-theater operations as directly analogous to Shapiro's dual-mandate challenge. McChrystal's 'Team of Teams' model — building bridges between specialized units that traditionally operate independently — is the core value proposition. dossiers.recommended_meeting_framing: Frame the first meeting as a 'leadership transition playbook' conversation — not a pitch. McChrystal Group has helped organizations navigate simultaneous leadership change, PE integration, and portfolio restructuring. The opening should acknowledge that WME Group's situation is unique in entertainment but that the underlying organizational dynamics — building shared consciousness across newly independent units, transitioning from founder-led to professionally managed, creating alignment under PE ownership constraints — are challenges McChrystal has addressed across defense, finance, and technology. Offer a 90-minute diagnostic session focused on mapping the current organizational friction points between WME, 160over90, and IMG Licensing, with a deliverable Shapiro can use immediately. Avoid any suggestion that the culture is 'broken' — frame it as 'designing the next operating model for a fundamentally different company.'

emails.subject: Scaling leadership at the speed of AI demand
emails.body:
Gary,

Ciena's Q1 results were remarkable — 24% revenue growth, a $7B backlog, and the Vesta 200 launch redefining what's possible in optical networking. You're building the nervous system of the AI revolution.

But here's the pattern we see in hypergrowth environments: the infrastructure scales faster than the organization behind it. When a company goes from $4B to nearly $6B in two years, the leadership model that got you here rarely survives the next phase without deliberate redesign.

At McChrystal Group, we work with organizations navigating exactly this inflection — where the operational tempo has outpaced the decision-making architecture. Our Team of Teams framework was built for environments where speed and coordination aren't trade-offs but requirements.

With a new CFO onboarding, no permanent CHRO, and demand accelerating across hyperscale, cloud, and AI customers simultaneously, this is the moment to pressure-test whether your leadership structure is built for the next $6B — not just the last.

I'd welcome 20 minutes to share what we've seen work in other high-growth technology companies facing similar scaling challenges.

Best,
[Senior Partner]
McChrystal Group
emails.hook: Ciena's hypergrowth (24% YoY revenue, $7B backlog) is outpacing organizational capacity — new CFO, no CHRO, and AI-driven demand acceleration create a leadership scaling challenge.

emails.subject: What we learned helping a $5B tech company scale without breaking
emails.body:
Gary,

Following up on my earlier note. I wanted to share a pattern we've observed that may resonate given where Ciena sits today.

When technology companies hit the $5B–$10B range on a steep growth curve, the most common failure mode isn't product or market — it's coordination cost. Teams that operated fluidly at $3B start building walls. Decision authority that was clear becomes contested. The CEO ends up as the bottleneck for cross-functional trade-offs that should be resolved two levels down.

General McChrystal faced this exact challenge when Joint Special Operations Command scaled from hundreds of operations per month to thousands. The answer wasn't more hierarchy — it was shared consciousness: ensuring every node in the network had the context to act independently while remaining aligned.

We've applied this same model at companies navigating the kind of telecom-to-AI platform shift Ciena is executing. The framework is particularly relevant when you're integrating acquired capabilities (like Nubis) while simultaneously serving customers whose own architectures are being reimagined.

Happy to share the specific playbook if useful.

Best,
[Senior Partner]
McChrystal Group
emails.hook: Team of Teams framework directly applicable to coordination challenges in hypergrowth tech companies — shared consciousness model for decentralized execution.

emails.subject: A conversation about Ciena's next chapter
emails.body:
Gary,

One more thought — and then I'll get out of your inbox.

You've led Ciena for 25 years, navigating the telecom bust, the cloud build-out, and now the AI infrastructure boom. Very few CEOs have the institutional memory you carry. That's an enormous asset, but it also means the organization may be more dependent on your judgment than is sustainable at this scale.

The question I'd put to you: if you were to step away for 90 days, would every critical decision still get made at the right speed and quality? In our experience, the honest answer at most $6B companies in hypergrowth is no — and that's not a leadership failure, it's an organizational design problem.

I'd like to propose a 45-minute conversation where we walk through McChrystal Group's organizational readiness diagnostic — a structured assessment of decision-making velocity, information flow, and leadership bench depth. No pitch, just a candid exchange.

Would any time in the next two weeks work?

Best,
[Senior Partner]
McChrystal Group

emails.body:
Jason,

DraftKings' February restructuring announcement caught my attention — not because of the 5% workforce reduction (that's tactical), but because of what it signals strategically. You're simultaneously defending a $6.7B sports betting franchise, launching a federally regulated prediction markets platform, replacing human roles with AI agents, and resetting Wall Street expectations after guiding below consensus.

That's not a cost-cutting exercise. That's a fundamental organizational redesign under pressure.

At McChrystal Group, we specialize in exactly this: helping leadership teams execute multi-front transformations without losing the cultural cohesion that made them successful in the first place. Our work is rooted in General McChrystal's experience transforming JSOC — an organization that had to reinvent itself while still operating at full tempo in combat.

The parallel to DraftKings is striking. You can't pause sports betting operations while you build prediction markets. You can't slow down while you integrate AI into workflows. And you can't afford to lose the entrepreneurial DNA that built this company while you're professionalizing the operating model.

I'd welcome a conversation about how we've helped other high-growth companies navigate this kind of strategic pivot.

Best,
[Senior Partner]
McChrystal Group
emails.hook: DraftKings executing simultaneous restructuring (5% layoffs), prediction markets pivot, and AI agent replacement — multi-front transformation under Wall Street pressure.

emails.subject: The organizational cost of fighting on multiple fronts
emails.body:
Jason,

Building on my previous note — I wanted to share a framework that may be directly relevant to what DraftKings is navigating.

When organizations try to simultaneously defend a core business, launch a new growth engine, and restructure operations, the most common failure isn't strategy — it's organizational attention. Leaders get pulled in too many directions. Middle management doesn't know which priorities are real. And the people you most need to retain start wondering whether the company they joined still exists.

We call this the "multi-front tax" — and it compounds fast. The Chief Transformation Officer role you created was a smart structural move, but structure alone doesn't solve the coordination problem. You need a shared operating rhythm that keeps sports betting, prediction markets, and operational efficiency efforts synchronized without creating bureaucracy.

Our Team of Teams model was designed for exactly this. It creates what we call "shared consciousness" — a system where every team understands the broader context well enough to make autonomous decisions that serve the whole, not just their silo.

Given DraftKings' guidance reset and the market's reaction, speed of execution on this transformation isn't optional. I'd be glad to share how we've accelerated similar pivots.

Best,
[Senior Partner]
McChrystal Group
emails.hook: Multi-front transformation creates 'organizational attention' tax — Team of Teams framework solves coordination across competing strategic priorities.

emails.subject: 30 minutes on DraftKings' transformation playbook
emails.body:
Jason,

Final thought on this — DraftKings has accomplished something rare: building a $6.7B company in an industry that barely existed a decade ago. That required extraordinary speed, risk tolerance, and entrepreneurial instinct.

The challenge now is different. The prediction markets opportunity you've sized at $10B requires regulatory sophistication, operational discipline, and cross-functional integration — capabilities that don't emerge naturally from a startup culture, no matter how talented the team.

Meanwhile, your public comments about replacing human hires with AI agents signal a workforce transformation that will fundamentally change how DraftKings operates. That's the right strategic direction, but the execution risk is enormous if it's not paired with deliberate culture and leadership work.

I'd like to propose a focused 30-minute conversation to discuss two things: (1) how McChrystal Group has helped companies maintain cultural cohesion through workforce transformation, and (2) a specific diagnostic we run on organizational readiness for multi-front strategic pivots.

No generic pitch — just a direct conversation about what you're facing. Would any time work in the next couple of weeks?

Best,
[Senior Partner]
McChrystal Group

emails.subject: Leading through Lululemon's inflection point
emails.body:
Meghan,

The leadership challenge you've stepped into at Lululemon is one of the most consequential in retail right now. An interim co-CEO structure following Calvin McDonald's departure, Elliott Management's $1B activist stake, Chip Wilson's proxy fight, and four C-suite exits — all while running an $11B global brand that still needs to perform every quarter.

This isn't a CEO search problem. It's an organizational resilience problem.

At McChrystal Group, we work with leadership teams navigating exactly this kind of high-stakes transition — where the pressure is coming from every direction simultaneously and the margin for error is measured in weeks, not quarters.

General McChrystal built our firm around a principle learned in combat: in volatile, uncertain environments, the organization's ability to adapt faster than the situation deteriorates is the only thing that matters. That principle applies directly to what Lululemon faces today.

The decisions you and André make in the next 90 days — on organizational structure, leadership appointments, and strategic direction — will define the company's trajectory for years. We've helped leadership teams in similar transitions make those decisions with clarity and speed.

I'd welcome a brief conversation about how we might support you during this critical period.

Best,
[Senior Partner]
McChrystal Group

emails.subject: What we've learned about co-CEO transitions and activist pressure
emails.body:
Meghan,

Following up with a thought that may be directly useful given the co-CEO structure you and André are operating under.

In our experience, co-CEO arrangements work when two conditions are met: (1) decision rights are surgically clear — not just on paper, but in practice, and (2) the broader leadership team and board have a shared operating picture of what success looks like in the transition period.

Without both, co-CEO structures create ambiguity that cascades through the organization. Store managers, regional leaders, and product teams start hedging — waiting for clarity rather than executing. In a retail environment where speed-to-market and brand coherence are everything, that hesitation is costly.

Add Elliott's activist pressure and Chip Wilson's public involvement, and the external noise makes internal alignment even harder. Your teams need to hear one clear signal, not three competing ones.

McChrystal Group's crisis leadership framework is designed for exactly this — establishing a shared operating rhythm that cuts through ambiguity and gives every leader in the organization the context to act decisively. We've deployed this in post-M&A integrations, CEO transitions, and activist defense situations.

Happy to share specific examples if they'd be helpful.

Best,
[Senior Partner]
McChrystal Group
emails.hook: Co-CEO structures fail without surgically clear decision rights and shared operating picture — crisis leadership framework prevents organizational hesitation during transition.

emails.subject: A proposal for Lululemon's leadership transition
emails.body:
Meghan,

One final note. Whether the board selects Elliott's candidate, an internal successor, or someone else entirely, the bigger question remains: what organizational model does Lululemon need for its next chapter?

The Breezethrough product recall, the inventory discipline issues, and the C-suite departures weren't random events — they're symptoms of an organization that outgrew its operating model. The next CEO will inherit those structural challenges regardless of their individual brilliance.

McChrystal Group has a specific engagement model for leadership transitions at scale. It includes three components: (1) an organizational readiness assessment that maps decision-making patterns, information flow, and cultural fault lines; (2) a 90-day leadership alignment sprint that establishes the operating rhythm for the new leadership team; and (3) ongoing advisory support during execution.

I'd like to propose a 45-minute conversation — ideally including you and Marti Morfitt — to discuss whether this approach could help Lululemon navigate the transition more effectively. We can share case studies from similar situations and be completely direct about whether we think there's a fit.

Would any time in the next two weeks work?

McChrystal Group

emails.subject: Building SAIC's next operating model
emails.body:
Jim,

Congratulations on the permanent CEO appointment. The board's unanimous decision after a thorough search speaks volumes — especially given the complexity of what SAIC is navigating right now.

You're executing a 5-to-3 group consolidation, integrating SilverEdge's $205M acquisition, reprogramming $100M in indirect spend, and doing it all while the defense and government services landscape faces unprecedented uncertainty from DOGE-driven budget scrutiny.

That's a transformation with a very short fuse.

McChrystal Group was founded to help organizations operating in exactly these conditions — high stakes, compressed timelines, and environments where the rules are changing faster than the bureaucracy can adapt. General McChrystal built these principles transforming military organizations, and our firm has since applied them across the defense industrial base and federal services sector.

The 5-to-3 consolidation is the right structural move, but structure alone won't deliver the $100M in reprogrammed spend or the SilverEdge integration. Those require a leadership operating model that's as streamlined as the new org chart.

With your nearly four decades in the federal sector, you understand this intuitively. I'd welcome a conversation about how McChrystal Group might accelerate the integration and operating model work.

Best,
[Senior Partner]
McChrystal Group

emails.subject: Lessons from military-to-mission integration
emails.body:
Jim,

Building on my earlier note — I wanted to share a perspective on the SilverEdge integration that may be relevant.

When defense services companies acquire smaller, mission-focused firms, the most common failure isn't technology integration — it's cultural absorption. SilverEdge's value lies in its agility, its SaaS-native approach (the SOAR platform), and its deep relationships with the Intelligence Community. Those are precisely the qualities that large-company integration processes tend to destroy.

General McChrystal faced a similar challenge when JSOC needed to integrate capabilities from CIA, NSA, and FBI into a unified operational framework. The answer wasn't forcing everyone into one hierarchy — it was creating a networked model where each unit retained its distinctive capabilities while gaining access to the broader organization's resources.

Our Team of Teams framework is designed for exactly this integration challenge. It preserves the acquired company's operational DNA while building the connective tissue needed for cross-portfolio leverage — which is exactly what you described in the Q3 earnings call when you talked about deploying SilverEdge's people and solutions across the SAIC portfolio.

I'd also note that John Tien on your board brings deep experience in interagency coordination from his Army service and DHS role — he'd likely recognize the parallels immediately.

Best,
[Senior Partner]
McChrystal Group
emails.hook: SilverEdge integration risks cultural absorption — Team of Teams model preserves acquired company's agility while enabling cross-portfolio leverage.

emails.subject: A focused conversation on SAIC's integration and restructuring
emails.body:
Jim,

One final thought. The DOGE environment creates a dynamic that most defense services firms haven't faced before: simultaneous pressure to reduce costs, demonstrate efficiency, and maintain mission readiness — all under intense public scrutiny.

Your $100M indirect spend reprogramming and organizational consolidation position SAIC well on the efficiency front. But the organizations that thrive in this environment won't just be leaner — they'll be fundamentally faster at decision-making, customer engagement, and cross-functional coordination.

McChrystal Group has a specific diagnostic we've run with defense and government services organizations facing similar pressures. It maps decision-making velocity across the organization, identifies bottlenecks in information flow, and benchmarks leadership alignment against the pace of external change.

For SAIC, this diagnostic would be particularly valuable in two areas: (1) validating that the 5-to-3 consolidation is eliminating decision layers rather than just renaming them, and (2) ensuring the SilverEdge integration is creating operational synergy rather than bureaucratic overhead.

I'd like to propose a 30-minute conversation — happy to include anyone from your leadership team you think would benefit. Would any time in the next two weeks work?

Best,
[Senior Partner]
McChrystal Group

emails.subject: Leading through Imperial's most significant transformation
emails.body:
Dan,

Imperial Oil's restructuring announcement — 20% workforce reduction, Calgary exit, Edmonton consolidation, and offshoring to ExxonMobil's global centres — is the most significant organizational transformation in the company's recent history. The $150M in annual cost savings and CAD $330M restructuring charge signal the scope.

But the real challenge isn't the restructuring plan itself. It's executing it while maintaining operational excellence at Kearl, Cold Lake, Strathcona, and Sarnia — facilities where safety, environmental performance, and production targets don't pause for corporate reorganization.

At McChrystal Group, we work with organizations navigating exactly this: high-stakes operational environments undergoing fundamental restructuring. General McChrystal built our firm on the principle that transformation and execution are not sequential — they must happen simultaneously, which requires a leadership model designed for that dual mandate.

Your military background gives you a natural understanding of this. Leading through uncertainty while maintaining operational discipline is a core military competency — and one that translates directly to what Imperial needs right now.

I'd welcome a conversation about how McChrystal Group has helped other energy companies execute large-scale restructuring without sacrificing operational performance.

Best,
[Senior Partner]
McChrystal Group
emails.hook: Imperial Oil's 20% workforce reduction and Calgary-to-Edmonton relocation is the largest restructuring in company history — execution risk is enormous for ongoing operations.

emails.subject: What restructuring gets wrong — and how to fix it
emails.body:
Dan,

Following up on my earlier note with a pattern we've observed in large-scale workforce restructurings that may be directly relevant to Imperial.

The most common failure mode isn't the restructuring itself — it's the 18-month shadow period between announcement and completion. Imperial has told 900 people their roles are going away by 2027, while asking many of them to continue performing at a high level in safety-critical operations for another two years.

That creates a leadership challenge that spreadsheet-driven restructuring plans don't address: how do you maintain engagement, retain critical knowledge, and preserve safety culture when a significant portion of your workforce is simultaneously processing their own career transition?

Add the complexity of relocating remaining roles from Calgary to the Strathcona Refinery — effectively asking people to uproot their lives — and you have a cultural change challenge layered on top of an operational one.

McChrystal Group's approach to transformation is rooted in what we call "shared consciousness" — ensuring that every leader in the organization, from the C-suite to front-line supervisors, has the same understanding of why changes are happening, what success looks like, and how their specific role contributes to the outcome.

This isn't communication planning. It's operational architecture for change. Happy to share specifics if useful.

Best,
[Senior Partner]
McChrystal Group
emails.hook: 18-month shadow period between restructuring announcement and completion creates engagement, knowledge retention, and safety culture risks at operating sites.

emails.subject: Supporting Imperial through the transition — a specific proposal
emails.body:
Dan,

One final thought. As Imperial consolidates into Edmonton and integrates more deeply with ExxonMobil's global centres, the company faces a fundamental identity question: how does a 145-year-old Canadian institution maintain its operational culture and institutional knowledge while becoming more tightly integrated with its parent company's global operating model?

This isn't an abstract question. It directly affects retention of key operators, the effectiveness of offshored functions, and whether the Edmonton-consolidated organization operates with the cohesion and speed that Imperial's asset base demands.

McChrystal Group has a specific engagement model for organizations executing large-scale restructuring in high-stakes operating environments. It includes three elements: (1) a leadership alignment diagnostic that identifies where decision-making authority needs to shift during the transition, (2) a cultural integration framework for blending relocated, retained, and globally shared teams, and (3) an ongoing advisory cadence through the transition period.

I'd like to propose a 30-minute conversation to discuss whether this approach would be valuable for Imperial. Given CEO John Whelan's focus on maximizing asset value through this restructuring, we could also discuss how our framework ensures operational continuity during the transition.

Would any time in the next two weeks work?

Best,
[Senior Partner]
McChrystal Group

emails.subject: Post-Chapter 11 — building the leadership team for Wolfspeed's next era
emails.body:
Robert,

Congratulations on steering Wolfspeed through an expedited Chapter 11 and emerging with a dramatically healthier balance sheet. That was a feat of operational discipline.

The harder chapter starts now. You have a new board under Chairman Abate, a leaner workforce after cutting roughly 20% of headcount, and a 200mm silicon-carbide manufacturing transition that will test every layer of the organization. In our experience working with leadership teams navigating post-restructuring pivots, the technical strategy is rarely the bottleneck — it is aligning an entirely reconstituted leadership team around a shared operating rhythm while rebuilding trust with the people who stayed.

McChrystal Group has helped organizations in similar inflection points — new leadership teams, workforce reductions, high-stakes manufacturing transitions — build the cross-functional alignment and shared consciousness needed to execute at speed.

I would welcome a 30-minute conversation about how you are thinking about aligning your new leadership team around the 200mm transition. No pitch — just a candid exchange between people who have led through similar complexity.

Best regards,
McChrystal Group

emails.subject: A framework for post-restructuring leadership alignment
emails.body:
Robert,

Following up on my earlier note. I wanted to share a pattern we see repeatedly in post-restructuring turnarounds.

Organizations that emerge from Chapter 11 with new leadership teams typically face a 6-to-12-month window where the reconstituted team either builds genuine shared consciousness or defaults to siloed execution. The difference is rarely about talent — it is about operating rhythm. When you have a new CEO, a new board chairman with deep defense and technology experience like Anthony Abate, and leaders who did not choose each other, the natural tendency is to stay in lanes and avoid friction. That is exactly when misalignment compounds.

General McChrystal faced an analogous challenge in 2003 — integrating operators, analysts, and intelligence professionals from different agencies who had never worked together into a single team executing dozens of operations per night. The solution was not reorganizing the org chart. It was building shared consciousness through radical information-sharing and empowered execution at the edge.

We have codified that approach for commercial organizations navigating exactly the kind of transition Wolfspeed is in. I have attached a short overview of our Team of Teams operating model — I think you will find the parallels to your current situation striking.

Best regards,
McChrystal Group
emails.hook: Wolfspeed's new board and C-suite must gel quickly while executing a complex manufacturing transition — a situation where the 'Team of Teams' operating model directly applies.

emails.subject: Proposal: leadership alignment session for Wolfspeed's executive team
emails.body:
Robert,

I will be direct. Wolfspeed is in the kind of moment where a focused leadership alignment engagement — two days with your top team — can compress months of organic team-building into a structured process with clear outcomes.

Here is what I would propose: a confidential diagnostic of your current leadership operating rhythm, followed by a facilitated session with your executive team and key board members. We would focus on three things:

1. Mapping the decision architecture for the 200mm transition — who owns what, where authority sits, and where information needs to flow faster
2. Identifying the friction points between your reconstituted C-suite, the new board, and the manufacturing operations teams
3. Building a 90-day operating cadence that creates shared consciousness without bureaucratic overhead

We have done this with semiconductor and advanced manufacturing companies navigating similar complexity, and the typical outcome is measurably faster decision-making and a leadership team that operates as a genuine team rather than a collection of functional heads.

Would a 30-minute call next week make sense to explore whether this is the right fit? I am happy to work around your schedule.

Best regards,
McChrystal Group

emails.subject: From regional to category-led — the organizational challenge behind Haleon's six-OU model
emails.body:
Brian,

The operating model evolution you announced — moving from regional to six category-led Operating Units with new Chief Growth Officer and Chief Transformation Officer roles — is one of the most ambitious structural changes in consumer healthcare. The GBP 175-200M savings target signals the board's conviction that this is not incremental.

Here is what I have seen in similar transformations: the org chart change is the easy part. The hard part is rewiring how 24,000 people make decisions when the authority structure shifts from geography to category. Regional leaders who previously owned their P&L now report into Operating Unit heads. Matrix relationships multiply. Without deliberate redesign of the operating rhythm, the new model creates more silos than it eliminates.

McChrystal Group has partnered with global consumer companies navigating exactly this kind of transformation — from regional to functional or category-led models — helping leadership teams build the cross-functional alignment that makes the new structure actually work.

I would welcome the chance to compare notes on how you are thinking about the human side of this transition. Would a brief conversation be useful?

Best regards,
McChrystal Group

emails.subject: How 'shared consciousness' prevents matrix paralysis
emails.body:
Brian,

A quick follow-up with a thought that may be relevant as your team designs the operating rhythm for the new six-OU model.

Your 'Win as One' strategy is the right aspiration. The risk is that splitting EMEA & LatAm into three separate Operating Units, while creating an independent India Subcontinent unit, actually increases organizational seams rather than reducing them. Category-led models work brilliantly when information flows horizontally across OUs. They stall when each OU optimizes locally.

General McChrystal confronted a structurally identical problem — a task force composed of distinct units (SEALs, Army Rangers, intelligence agencies) that needed to operate as one team despite different missions, cultures, and chains of command. The breakthrough was not structural — it was creating shared consciousness through radical transparency and embedding liaisons who carried context across boundaries.

We have translated that approach into a commercial framework that consumer and healthcare companies use to prevent matrix paralysis during exactly the kind of transition Haleon is executing. The Chief Transformation Officer role Jonathan Workman has taken on is critical — and our experience suggests the first 90 days of that role determine whether transformation is a program or a permanent capability.

Happy to share more detail if useful.

Best regards,
McChrystal Group

2. A facilitated session with your executive team and OU leaders to build the shared operating rhythm that makes 'Win as One' operational rather than aspirational

We would work closely with Jonathan Workman and his transformation team to ensure this integrates with the broader program rather than adding another workstream.

This is the kind of engagement where two days of focused work with the right people in the room can save six months of organic friction. Would a conversation next week make sense?

emails.subject: Leading through existential uncertainty — Trinseo's inflection point
emails.body:
Frank,

I will be candid: Trinseo is navigating the kind of moment that defines a CEO's legacy. Active debt restructuring discussions with bankruptcy as a possibility. A workforce cut nearly in half. Plant closures in Italy and Germany. And a 48% Glassdoor approval rate that tells you the people who remained are watching closely to see whether this leadership team can be trusted to lead them through.

This is not a strategy problem. It is a leadership and culture problem operating under extreme financial pressure. The employees still at Trinseo — 3,100 people across the US and EMEA — need to see a credible path forward from a leadership team that communicates with clarity, makes decisions with speed, and rebuilds trust that has been eroded by years of contraction.

McChrystal Group was founded for exactly these moments. We work with leadership teams in high-stakes, high-ambiguity environments where the margin for error is thin and the cost of organizational dysfunction is existential. General McChrystal built our approach leading teams through precisely this kind of complexity.

I would welcome a confidential conversation about how you are thinking about leading through this period. No pitch — just a candid exchange with someone who has seen this pattern before.

Best regards,
McChrystal Group
emails.hook: Trinseo is in active bankruptcy discussions as of February 2026, has cut its workforce from 6,000+ to 3,100, carries a 48% Glassdoor recommendation rate, and faces a US-EMEA cultural divide — a confluence of crises where leadership credibility is the only asset that cannot be restructured on paper.

emails.subject: Bridging the US-EMEA divide under pressure
emails.body:
Frank,

Following up on my earlier note. I wanted to flag something specific that I suspect is already on your radar but may be harder to address than the financial restructuring.

Trinseo's decision to merge Engineered Materials, Plastics Solutions, and Polystyrene into a consolidated unit — while closing plants in Italy and Germany — sends a particular message to your European workforce. Combine that with the existing US-EMEA cultural divide and a Glassdoor score that suggests trust is thin, and you have a recipe for the kind of quiet disengagement that makes the $30M EBITDA savings target unreachable.

We see this pattern frequently in global manufacturing companies under financial stress: the restructuring math works on paper, but the people who have to execute it have already mentally checked out. The remaining 3,100 employees are your restructuring plan — without their discretionary effort, the savings evaporate.

General McChrystal faced a version of this bridging US and coalition forces with fundamentally different cultures, incentives, and levels of trust. The approach — building shared consciousness across organizational and cultural boundaries — is directly applicable to what Trinseo needs right now.

I would be happy to share how we have helped global manufacturers rebuild trust and alignment during periods of similar uncertainty.

Best regards,
McChrystal Group
emails.hook: Trinseo's consolidation of three business units and closure of European plants deepens an existing US-EMEA cultural divide that could undermine the $30M EBITDA savings target.

emails.subject: A focused engagement for Trinseo's leadership team
emails.body:
Frank,

I recognize your bandwidth is extraordinarily constrained right now. So I will be specific about what I think could make the biggest difference in the shortest time.

I would propose a confidential, two-day leadership alignment engagement for your top team — Paula Cooney and the CHRO function would be a critical participant. The focus:

1. Crisis leadership operating cadence — how your leadership team communicates, decides, and executes during the restructuring period, with specific attention to the US-EMEA divide
2. Workforce trust diagnostic — a rapid, honest assessment of where your remaining 3,100 employees stand and what it will take to convert survivors into committed rebuilders
3. 90-day communication and decision framework — a concrete operating rhythm that ensures your consolidated business unit structure drives the $30M savings rather than creating new friction

This is not a multi-month consulting engagement. It is a focused intervention at a moment when leadership alignment is the difference between a successful restructuring and a slow decline.

Would a confidential 30-minute call this week or next make sense? I am flexible on timing and understand the sensitivity of the situation.

Best regards,
McChrystal Group

emails.subject: After the 40% cut — making Block's AI-first model actually work
emails.body:
Amrita,

Block's decision to reduce headcount by 40% while restructuring from a business-unit to a functional model is one of the boldest organizational bets in recent tech history. The market clearly agrees — the 24% stock surge reflects confidence that a leaner, AI-augmented Block can deliver on the 18% gross profit growth and 54% profit increase you have guided.

Here is what concerns me about the execution. You are asking a workforce that just watched 4,000 colleagues leave to simultaneously adopt an AI-first operating model, learn new reporting relationships under a functional structure, and maintain the trust and compliance infrastructure that a financial services company cannot operate without. That is three transformations in one, and each amplifies the risk of the others.

McChrystal Group works with organizations navigating exactly this kind of compressed, high-stakes transformation — where the strategic logic is sound but the organizational execution determines whether it delivers. We bring a leadership and operating model framework built in environments where the margin for error was measured in lives, not quarters.

I would welcome a conversation about how you are thinking about the human side of Block's transformation. Your Fortune interview was compelling — I think there is more to discuss.

Best regards,
McChrystal Group
emails.hook: Block cut 4,000+ employees (nearly half its workforce) in February 2026 in the largest AI-justified restructuring in tech history, while simultaneously shifting from a business-unit to functional model — two massive organizational changes that compound each other's risk.

emails.subject: The 'Team of Teams' operating model for AI-first organizations
emails.body:
Amrita,

A quick follow-up with a framework I think is directly relevant to Block's transformation.

Jack's vision of smaller, highly talented teams using AI to automate work is structurally similar to what General McChrystal built at Joint Special Operations Command — small, empowered teams executing at the edge with shared access to intelligence and a common operating picture. The parallel is not superficial: both models require radical information-sharing, clear decision rights, and trust that the teams closest to the problem have the authority to act.

The risk in Block's current transition is that the shift from business-unit to functional model — combined with halving the workforce — creates exactly the opposite dynamic. Functional models, without deliberate cross-functional operating rhythms, default to vertical silos. Teams that used to own end-to-end outcomes now depend on handoffs. AI tools like Goose accelerate individual workflows but do not solve the coordination problem between teams.

We have codified the Team of Teams approach into a framework that technology companies use to maintain speed and agility through exactly these kinds of structural transitions. The organizations that get this right — deliberate operating rhythm, clear decision architecture, shared consciousness — are the ones where the AI productivity gains actually compound.

Happy to share more detail if this resonates.

Best regards,
McChrystal Group
emails.hook: Block's shift to a functional model with smaller, AI-augmented teams mirrors the decentralized, empowered-execution model McChrystal Group pioneered — but without deliberate design, functional models create new silos.

emails.subject: Proposal: leadership alignment for Block's post-restructuring operating model
emails.body:
Amrita,

I will be direct about what I think would be most valuable for Block right now.

You described this as a "two-year journey." The next 90 days will determine the trajectory. Your remaining team is absorbing the loss of 4,000 colleagues, learning a new functional structure, and integrating AI tools into daily workflows — simultaneously. The leaders who now own cross-functional outcomes need a shared operating cadence that did not exist in the previous business-unit model.

I would propose a focused engagement:

1. Decision architecture mapping — identifying where authority sits in the new functional model, where information needs to flow faster between Square, Cash App, and TIDAL, and where AI automation creates coordination gaps
2. Leadership alignment session — a facilitated session with your cross-functional leadership team to build the shared consciousness and operating rhythm the new model requires
3. 90-day execution cadence — a concrete framework for how the post-restructuring Block operates, decides, and communicates

This is designed to be high-impact and low-burden — a two-day engagement with your top team, not a multi-month program. Given the pace at which Block moves, that approach should fit your operating style.

Would a 30-minute call make sense to explore whether this is the right fit?

Best regards,
McChrystal Group
